steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Rhetorical Synthesis question. The student wants to make a generalization about the kind of study Glickman, Brown, and Song conducted — not to report a specific finding. That means the correct answer must describe the research method and field broadly, not repeat case-specific data from the notes.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Based on statistical analysis, Glickman, Brown, and Song claim that John Lennon wrote the verse of ‘In My Life.’": This misrepresents the finding (they found only an 18.9% probability for McCartney; they did not claim Lennon definitively wrote it) and reports a specific case detail rather than generalizing the type of study. ✗
  • Choice B — "There is only an 18.9% probability that Paul McCartney wrote the verse for ‘In My Life’; John Lennon is the more likely author.": This reports the specific numerical result from the notes. It summarizes a finding, not the broader kind of study. ✗
  • Choice C — "It is likely that John Lennon, not Paul McCartney, wrote the verse for ‘In My Life.’": This is a specific conclusion about one song, not a generalization about the research approach. ✗
  • Choice D — "Researchers have used statistical methods to address questions of authorship within the field of music.": This steps back from the specific case and characterizes the methodology (statistical methods) and the research domain (authorship in music) — exactly the generalization requested. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
